lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Tut! I have the best armour of the world. Would it were day!

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: You have an excellent armour; but let my horse have his due.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: It is the best horse of Europe.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: Will it never be morning?

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: My Lord of Orleans, and my lord high constable, you talk of horse and armour?

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: You are as well provided of both as any prince in the world.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: What a long night is this! I will not change my horse

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: with any that treads but on four pasterns. Ça, ha! he bounds

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: from the earth, as if his entrails were hairs; le cheval volant,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: the Pegasus, qui a les narines de feu! When I bestride him,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: I soar, I am a hawk: he trots the air; the earth

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: sings when he touches it; the basest horn of his hoof is

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: more musical than the pipe of Hermes.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: He’s of the colour of the nutmeg.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: And of the heat of the ginger. It is a beast for

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Perseus: he is pure air and fire; and the dull elements of

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: earth and water never appear in him, but only in patient stillness

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: while his rider mounts him: he is indeed a horse; and all

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: other jades you may call beasts.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Indeed, my lord, it is a most absolute and excellent horse.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: It is the prince of palfreys; his neigh is like the bidding

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: of a monarch and his countenance enforces homage.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: No more, cousin.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Nay, the man hath no wit that cannot, from the rising of

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: the lark to the lodging of the lamb, vary deserved praise on

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: my palfrey: it is a theme as fluent as the sea: turn

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: the sands into eloquent tongues, and my horse is argument for them

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: all: ’tis a subject for a sovereign to reason on, and for

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: a sovereign’s sovereign to ride on; and for the world, familiar to

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: us and unknown, to lay apart their particular functions and wonder at

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: him. I once writ a sonnet in his praise and began thus:

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: “Wonder of nature,”—

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: I have heard a sonnet begin so to one’s mistress.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Then did they imitate that which I composed to my courser, for

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: my horse is my mistress.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: Your mistress bears well.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Me well; which is the prescript praise and perfection of a good and particular mistress.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Nay, for methought yesterday your mistress shrewdly shook your back.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: So perhaps did yours.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Mine was not bridled.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: O then belike she was old and gentle; and you rode, like

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: a kern of Ireland, your French hose off, and in your strait

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: strossers.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: You have good judgment in horsemanship.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Be warned by me, then: they that ride so and ride not

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: warily, fall into foul bogs. I had rather have my horse to

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: my mistress.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: I had as lief have my mistress a jade.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: I tell thee, constable, my mistress wears his own hair.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: I could make as true a boast as that, if I had

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: a sow to my mistress.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: “Le chien est retourné à son propre vomissement, et la truie lavée

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: au bourbier:” thou makest use of anything.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Yet do I not use my horse for my mistress, or any

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: such proverb so little kin to the purpose.

lines.speaker: RAMBURES
lines.text: My lord constable, the armour that I saw in your tent tonight,

lines.speaker: RAMBURES
lines.text: are those stars or suns upon it?

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Stars, my lord.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Some of them will fall tomorrow, I hope.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: And yet my sky shall not want.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: That may be, for you bear a many superfluously, and ’twere more

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: honour some were away.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Even as your horse bears your praises; who would trot as well,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: were some of your brags dismounted.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Would I were able to load him with his desert! Will it

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: never be day? I will trot tomorrow a mile, and my way

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: shall be paved with English faces.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: I will not say so, for fear I should be faced out

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: of my way: but I would it were morning; for I would

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: fain be about the ears of the English.

lines.speaker: RAMBURES
lines.text: Who will go to hazard with me for twenty prisoners?

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: You must first go yourself to hazard, ere you have them.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: ’Tis midnight; I’ll go arm myself.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: The Dauphin longs for morning.

lines.speaker: RAMBURES
lines.text: He longs to eat the English.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: I think he will eat all he kills.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: By the white hand of my lady, he’s a gallant prince.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Swear by her foot, that she may tread out the oath.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: He is simply the most active gentleman of France.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Doing is activity; and he will still be doing.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: He never did harm, that I heard of.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Nor will do none tomorrow: he will keep that good name still.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: I know him to be valiant.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: I was told that by one that knows him better than you.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: What’s he?

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Marry, he told me so himself; and he said he cared not who knew it.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: He needs not; it is no hidden virtue in him.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: By my faith, sir, but it is; never anybody saw it but

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: his lackey: ’tis a hooded valour; and when it appears, it will

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: bate.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: Ill will never said well.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: I will cap that proverb with “There is flattery in friendship.”

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: And I will take up that with “Give the devil his due.”

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Well placed: there stands your friend for the devil: have at the

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: very eye of that proverb with “A pox of the devil.”

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: You are the better at proverbs, by how much “A fool’s bolt is soon shot.”

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: You have shot over.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: ’Tis not the first time you were overshot.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: My lord high constable, the English lie within fifteen hundred paces of your tents.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Who hath measured the ground?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: The Lord Grandpré.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: A valiant and most expert gentleman. Would it were day! Alas, poor

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Harry of England! he longs not for the dawning as we do.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: What a wretched and peevish fellow is this king of England, to

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: mope with his fat-brained followers so far out of his knowledge!

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: If the English had any apprehension, they would run away.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: That they lack; for if their heads had any intellectual armour, they

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: could never wear such heavy headpieces.

lines.speaker: RAMBURES
lines.text: That island of England breeds very valiant creatures; their mastiffs are of unmatchable courage.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: Foolish curs, that run winking into the mouth of a Russian bear

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: and have their heads crushed like rotten apples! You may as well

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: say, that’s a valiant flea that dare eat his breakfast on the

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: lip of a lion.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Just, just; and the men do sympathize with the mastiffs in robustious

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: and rough coming on, leaving their wits with their wives: and then

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: give them great meals of beef and iron and steel, they will

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: eat like wolves and fight like devils.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: Ay, but these English are shrewdly out of beef.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Then shall we find tomorrow they have only stomachs to eat and

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: none to fight. Now is it time to arm: come, shall we

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: about it?

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: It is now two o’clock: but, let me see, by ten

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: We shall have each a hundred Englishmen.

stage_directions.text: Enter the Constable of France, the Lord Rambures, Orleans, Dauphin with others.

glosses.phrase: let my horse have his due
glosses.definition: credit my horse as well — Orleans begins the scene's running horse-bragging
the part of a horse's leg between the fetlock and hoof — 'he treads on four pasterns' = he's a true horse
French exclamation of delight — 'Ah, there!'
French: 'the flying horse' — mythical Pegasus
the winged horse of Greek myth, born from the blood of Medusa

glosses.phrase: qui a les narines de feu
glosses.definition: French: 'which has nostrils of fire'
he trots through the sky
the pipe Hermes used to charm Argus to sleep — Dauphin's mythological flattery of his horse
the tan-brown of a nutmeg
a mount fit for Perseus — the Greek hero who rode Pegasus
composed only of the two lighter, nobler classical elements
worn-out or ordinary horses
a lady's riding horse — the Dauphin strangely feminizes his war-mount
command of a king — the horse's whinny

glosses.phrase: rising of the lark to the lodging of the lamb
glosses.definition: from dawn to night — 'lark' sings at sunrise, 'lamb' lies down at dusk
the Dauphin wrote a love-sonnet to his horse — the joke's pivot

glosses.phrase: sonnet begin so to one’s mistress
glosses.definition: the customary target of such verse — Orleans catches the Dauphin writing his horse as his lover
carries the rider well — Orleans picks up the double meaning: bears (tolerates) and bears (carries)
severely jarred your back — with crude double meaning about sexual activity
controlled — bawdy pun
an Irish light-armed foot-soldier, proverbially bare-legged
without breeches — mocking the rider's undress
tight trousers/hose; here, nothing — 'strossers' = trousers
a nag; also slang for a loose woman — Constable sustains the Dauphin's mistress/horse conceit in the crudest direction
has her own hair (not a wig) — Dauphin's proud claim for his horse

glosses.phrase: Le chien est retourné à son propre vomissement
glosses.definition: French: 'The dog is returned to his own vomit' — from 2 Peter 2:22 / Proverbs 26:11; the Dauphin quotes scripture to insult the Constable
French: 'the washed sow to the mire' — second half of the same biblical proverb
heraldic devices on the armour — the banter segues from horses to escutcheons
more than necessary; extravagantly

glosses.phrase: fain be about the ears of the English
glosses.definition: gladly be at grips with the English
dice-gambling — a popular Elizabethan game of chance
the hour before Agincourt; the French still boast while the English pray

glosses.phrase: Swear by her foot, that she may tread out the oath
glosses.definition: Constable's indecent joke about the mistress's foot
courage kept hooded like a hawk — invisible until release, implied useless
hawking term: a hawk 'bates' when it beats its wings; also 'diminish' — Constable's pun
Orleans's proverb; Constable caps it
proverbial: a fool spends his wit quickly — fools have little ammunition
sulk among his dull-witted men
mental armour — brains that would explain their thick helmets
English mastiff dogs, proverbially courageous — Rambures's back-handed compliment
proverbially fierce — the bear-baiting image, English mastiffs charging in
share the nature of mastiffs — English men like their dogs

glosses.phrase: meals of beef and iron and steel
glosses.definition: beef and sword-steel — Elizabethan cliché of English diet and arms
severely short of beef — the English army is starving
let's get to arming — scene breaks toward battle

annotations.title: The French nobles and their horses — the play's critique of aristocratic vanity
annotations.body: The extended discussion of horses and armour among the French nobles the night before Agincourt is the play's sharpest piece of character-comedy. The Dauphin's description of his horse ('pure air and fire,' 'le cheval volant,' the Pegasus-comparison, 'my horse is my mistress') is over-refined to the point of absurdity. The Constable's needling about the Dauphin's sonnet-to-his-horse is a scholarly joke — sonnets are properly addressed to mistresses. The scene stages the French aristocracy as over-cultivated, effeminate (they prefer horses to women), and over-confident. This is not neutral characterization. Shakespeare is shaping the English self-image by contrast: the English are starving, dirty, and silent; the French are ornate, voluble, and dissipated. Historically, the French nobility at Agincourt were heavily armoured knights who were indeed beaten by English yeoman archers. The play translates that military reality into a cultural one. Virgil asks the modern reader to notice this: the comic French scenes are ideological work, not decoration. The culture being defeated is coded as feminine and aesthetic; the culture doing the defeating is coded as plain and masculine.
sources: Lisa Jardine, Still Harping on Daughters (1983)

annotations.title: The mastiffs and the poor English army
annotations.body: The French nobles' contempt for the English army is characterized with precision at the scene's close. Rambures compares the English to their 'mastiffs' — courageous but stupid dogs — and Orleans develops the simile: 'Foolish curs, that run winking into the mouth of a Russian bear / And have their heads crushed like rotten apples.' The Constable caps it: the English 'leave their wits with their wives, … give them great meals of beef and iron and steel, / They will eat like wolves and fight like devils.' Then the final deflation: 'Ay, but these English are shrewdly out of beef.' This is a clean dramatic irony. The audience knows the English have just been starved by the march; the French think they know the same and consider it the guarantee of victory. Tomorrow the army will win Agincourt. The scene is structured so that the French express their strategic assessment with perfect cultural snobbery and complete confidence, the audience hears it without commentary, and the play proceeds to falsify every word.
sources: Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

trials.prompt: How does the play characterize the French nobles at 3.7?
options: As over-refined, voluble, effeminate — in love with their horses and armour, composing sonnets to their mounts; this is ideological work, not neutral comedy. The play is shaping the English self-image by cultural contrast

trials.prompt: What is the joke of the Constable's line 'I have heard a sonnet begin so to one's mistress' (line 33)?
options: Sonnets are properly addressed to mistresses; the Dauphin has written one to his horse — the Constable is accusing him of treating his horse as a lover. The Dauphin then doubles down: 'my horse is my mistress'
The sonnet is a recent invention in the play's timeline

trials.prompt: What is the dramatic irony of the French nobles' confidence in 3.7?
options: They don't know that Henry has already left for Calais
They correctly assess the English army as starving and reduced, but conclude from this (wrongly) that victory is guaranteed — the audience knows Agincourt is tomorrow and the English will win
They are fooled by a false peace offer
The French king has died in the night
